title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who share their experiences with gardening or growing their own food. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Back to the Soil: Four People on Gardening and Growing Food

texts.A:
### Margaret, 62, Retired Librarian
[43]I have been growing vegetables in my back garden for over thirty years, long before it became fashionable.[/43] What started with a single row of courgettes has expanded into a fully productive plot that feeds my family from June to October. I grow everything from seed, which I find deeply satisfying, though it demands patience that not everyone has. [47]My proudest achievement was persuading the local primary school to let me set up a small growing area in their playground, so children could see where food actually comes from.[/47] [51]Several parents have told me their children now refuse to eat vegetables that haven't come from the school garden, which I find both funny and wonderful.[/51]
texts.B:
### Carlos, 38, Chef
Growing herbs and salad leaves on the restaurant's rooftop terrace was my head chef's idea, but I've become completely obsessed with it. [44]I never expected gardening to change the way I approach cooking, but having direct access to freshly cut herbs has made me far more experimental with flavours.[/44] I now visit the terrace every morning before service to see what's ready, and those few minutes of quiet have become the best part of my day. [48]Last spring, I started a small blog documenting the rooftop garden, which unexpectedly attracted thousands of followers interested in urban growing.[/48] [52]The biggest lesson the garden has taught me is that the most extraordinary dishes are often built around the simplest, freshest ingredients.[/52]
texts.C:
### Priya, 31, Architect
[45]I started growing tomatoes on my flat's south-facing balcony after my doctor suggested I needed more activities that would get me away from a screen.[/45] I had zero knowledge of plants and killed my first three batches before finally getting a crop I was proud of. What I didn't anticipate was how much I'd enjoy the problem-solving aspect – adjusting watering schedules, testing different compost mixes, researching companion planting. [49]I eventually connected with an allotment community nearby and now rent a small plot where I can grow on a much larger scale.[/49] Gardening has become a serious hobby rather than the casual stress reliever it was meant to be.
texts.D:
### Ben, 19, Student
[46]I got interested in growing food after watching a documentary about food poverty and feeling frustrated that I didn't know how to do anything practical in response.[/46] I started small – a few pots of chillies and herbs on my windowsill – but I quickly ran out of space. My university now hosts a community garden where students can apply for a raised bed, and I was lucky enough to get one. [50]I've been shocked by how much my food waste has reduced since I started growing things; you simply don't throw away something you've spent three months nurturing.[/50] I'd love to study sustainable agriculture eventually, though I'm studying engineering at the moment.

questions.question: has been practising this activity for a very long time, well before it became popular?
questions.explanation: Margaret has been growing vegetables for over thirty years, long before it became fashionable.

questions.question: was surprised to find that this activity influenced their approach to their professional work?
questions.explanation: Carlos never expected gardening to change the way he approaches cooking, but access to fresh herbs made him more experimental.

questions.question: was advised to take up gardening for health reasons?
questions.explanation: Priya started growing tomatoes after her doctor suggested activities to get her away from a screen.

questions.question: was motivated to start growing food by concern about a social issue?
questions.explanation: Ben became interested in growing food after watching a documentary about food poverty and wanting to do something practical.

questions.question: describes introducing gardening to children in an educational environment?
questions.explanation: Margaret persuaded a local primary school to set up a growing area so children could see where food comes from.

questions.question: found that sharing their gardening experience online attracted a significant audience?
questions.explanation: Carlos started a blog about the rooftop garden which unexpectedly attracted thousands of followers.

questions.question: expanded their gardening activity by joining an established community with dedicated land?
questions.explanation: Priya connected with an allotment community and now rents a small plot to grow on a larger scale.

questions.question: has noticed that growing their own food has changed their attitude towards throwing food away?
questions.explanation: Ben says his food waste has reduced significantly because you can't bring yourself to throw away something you've spent months growing.

questions.question: describes a humorous yet positive reaction from others as a result of their gardening efforts?
questions.explanation: Margaret finds it funny and wonderful that some children now refuse to eat vegetables not from the school garden.

questions.question: describes a broader insight about food that their gardening experience has given them?
questions.explanation: Carlos says the garden taught him that the most extraordinary dishes are built around the simplest, freshest ingredients.
